questions.question: 0.2 M NaOH solution means:
questions.question_raw: 0.2 M NaOH solution means:

options.text: 0.2 moles of NaOH in 1 litre of solution
0.2 moles of NaOH in 10 mL of solution
0.2 moles of NaOH in 0.5 litre of solution
0.2 moles of NaOH in 100 mL of solution

questions.explanation:
M represents the Molarity of a solution.
{tex}Molarity=\frac{No.\ of \ moles \ of \ solute}{1 \ liter \ of \ solution}{/tex}
The given strength of the NaOH solution confirms the above expression, hence the given answer is correct.

questions.question: A solution is prepared by adding 2 g of a substance A to 18 g of water. Calculate the mass percent of the solute?
questions.question_raw: A solution is prepared by adding 2 g of a substance A to 18 g of water. Calculate the mass percent of the solute?
questions.explanation:
Mass percent is calculated using the expression,
Mass percent = {tex}{ Weight \ of\ solute    \over  Weight\ of \ solution }\times 100{/tex}
 = {tex} {weight\ of\ solute  \over   weight\ of \ solute\ + \ weight\ of \ solvent }\times 100{/tex}
Substituting the given values in the above expression we get,
Mass percent = {tex}{ 2   \over  2 +18 }\times 100 {/tex}
= {tex}{ 2   \over  20 }\times 100 {/tex}
= 10.00 %

questions.question: If the concentration of glucose (C6H12O6) in the blood is 0.9 g L–1, what will be the molarity of glucose in the blood?
questions.question_raw: If the concentration of glucose (C6H12O6) in the blood is 0.9 g L–1, what will be the molarity of glucose in the blood?
questions.explanation:
Molar mass of glucose C6​H12​O6​=180 g/mol
concentration of glucose = 0.9 g/L
mass of glucose in 1 L =0.9 g
Molarity,{tex}M=\frac{\text { no. of moles }}{\text { volume in } L}{/tex}
no. of moles,n=mass/molar mass
{tex}n=\frac{0.9}{180}=5 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} mol
{tex}M=\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1}=0.005 M{/tex}

questions.question: A person is suffering with fever with a high temperature of 104°F. What will be his body temperature in°C?
questions.question_raw: A person is suffering with fever with a high temperature of 104°F. What will be his body temperature in°C?
questions.explanation:
The relation between the two scales of temperature (ie.°F & °C) is given by the expression:
The temperature in °F
= [ 9/5 (temperature in °C) + 32], or, temperature in °C
= [{(temperature in °F) - 32} {tex}\times{/tex} 5/9]
Substituting  104  for  the given temperature in o  F, we get,
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  The temperature in °C
=[(5/9) (104 - 32)]
= 40
Thus, the body temperature of  the person  in °C would be 40°C

questions.question: What is the mass of 1 molecule of O2?

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  1 mol of O2 gas containing 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023  molecules have a mass = 32.0 g
so 1 molecule would have a mass
= [ 32 / ( 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 ) ] g

questions.question:
When excess of CO is used for the reaction,
Fe2 O3(s) + 3CO(g) $\longrightarrow$ 2Fe + 3CO2 
the limiting reagent is_____.
questions.question_raw:
When excess of  CO is used for the reaction,
Fe2 O3(s) + 3CO(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}   2Fe + 3CO2 
the limiting reagent is_____.
questions.explanation:
As per stoichiometry  of the reaction,
Fe2 O3(s) + 3CO(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}   2Fe + 3CO2 
Fe2 O3   is the limiting reagent for the given reaction as it would be consumed first, because the other reagent ie. CO(g)  is used in excess.

questions.question:
For the reaction,
${\text{2}}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{l}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{l}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{3 }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 4}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }}\left( {\text{l}} \right),$
160 g N2H4 is mixed with 160 g N2O4, the yield of H2O is ________.
questions.question_raw:
For the reaction,
{tex}{\text{2}}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{l}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}\left( {\text{l}} \right){\text{ }} \to {\text{3 }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}\left( {\text{g}} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ 4}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O }}\left( {\text{l}} \right),{/tex}
160 g N2H4 is mixed with 160 g N2O4, the yield of H2O is ________.
questions.explanation:
Calculations:
Step 1/
As per stoichiometry of the given reaction,
{tex}2{N_2}{H_4}(l) + {N_2}{O_4}(l) \to 3{N_2}(g) + 4{H_2}O(l){/tex}
2 moles of N2H4 (l) reacts with 1 mol of N2O4 (l) to produce 3 moles of N2 (g) & 4 moles of H2O (l)
or, 32 g of N2H4 react with 92g of N2O4 (l) to produce 72g of N2(g) & 72g of H2O (l)
Now, the given masses of reactants are,
N2H4 =160 g.
or = {tex}\frac{160}{32}{/tex} mols = 5 moles
N2O4 = 160g or = {tex}\frac{160}{92}{/tex} moles = 1.739 moles
So, water produced in this reaction would depend upon the number of moles of N2O4 taken (calculated above = 1.739 mols), as it would be consumed first and is, therefore, the limiting reagent.
Step 2.
Since 1 mol of N2O4 yields water = 4 moles
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1.739 moles of N2O4 would yield water
= (1.739 {tex}\times{/tex} 4)moles
= (1.739 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 18)g = 125.208g
{tex}\approx{/tex} 125 g

questions.question: The number of moles of solute present in 1 kg of a solvent is called________.
questions.question_raw: The number of moles of solute present in 1 kg of a solvent is called________.
questions.explanation:
Molality is defined as no. of moles of solute present per kg of solvent. It is denoted as " m ". Mathematically,
Molality (m) = [number of moles of the solute] / kg of solvent
Since this mode of expressing the strength of a solution involves  (weight/weight) relationship of solute and solvent, the molality of the solution is not affected by variation in temperatures of the solution.

questions.question: Molality (m) of 3 M aqueous solution of NaCl is: (Given: Density of solution $=1.25 {~g} {~mL}^{-1}$, Molar mass in ${g} {mol}^{-1}$ : ${Na}-23, {Cl}-35.5$)
questions.question_raw: Molality (m) of 3 M aqueous solution of NaCl is: (Given: Density of solution {tex}=1.25 {~g} {~mL}^{-1}{/tex}, Molar mass in {tex}{g} {mol}^{-1}{/tex} : {tex}{Na}-23, {Cl}-35.5{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Since Molarity represents a number of moles of solute per litre of solution.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} moles of solute = 3 mols.
As, density of the solution is given
=  1.25 g/ mL
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of solution = 1250g
Mass of solvent = [1250 - 3 (58.5)]  g
= (1250 - 175.5) g
= 1074 .5 g
= 1.0745 Kg
Molality
= Number of moles of solute / Kg of solvent
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Molality = {tex}(\frac{3 mol}{1.0745 kg}){/tex} = 2.79 m

(M) = {tex}\frac{mass of solute}{molar mass of solute } \times {/tex}{tex}\frac{1000}{volume of solution in mL } {/tex} 

∴0.2​{tex}=\frac{w}{40 } \times {/tex}{tex}\frac{1000}{500} {/tex}

so, w = 4gm.
Amount of sodium hydroxide present in 500ml of 0.2M solutions is 4 gm.

questions.question_raw:
Consider the equation, Fe + H2SO4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe2(SO4)3 + H2
This equation can be balanced by inserting the following in blank spaces.
questions.explanation:
A balanced equation represents the same number of atoms of each element on both sides (i.e. LHS & RHS)  of the equation. The balanced equation is complied by introducing the coefficients as (2, 3, 1, 3) respectively. Thus the balanced equation for the given reaction is
2Fe + 3H2SO4 {tex}\to{/tex} Fe2 (SO4)3 + 3H2

questions.question:
For the reaction
2C4H10 (g)| + 1302 (g) $\rightarrow$ 8CO2 (g) + 10H20(l) 300 g of C4H10 is burnt in 1000 g of O2.
The yield of H2O and the limiting reagent in the reaction are
questions.question_raw:
For the reaction
2C4H10 (g)| + 1302 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 8CO2 (g) + 10H20(l) 300 g of C4H10 is burnt  in 1000 g of O2.
The yield  of H2O and the limiting reagent in the reaction are
questions.explanation:
Calculations  
Moles of C4H10 given
= {tex}\frac{300}{58}{/tex}
=   5.172 mol      
Moles of O2
= {tex}\frac{1000}{32}{/tex} moles
= 31.25 mol.
As per stoichiometry of the reaction,
since, 2 mol of C4H10 requires 13 mol of O2   
so 5.172 mol of C4H10 require mol of O2
=  {tex}\frac{5.172X 13}{2} {/tex} moles 
= 33.618 moles 
O2 we have is only 31.25 mol which lesser amount (ie. 33.618 mol )  than what is required, and hence would be consumed first.
Thus, O2 is  is limiting reagent.
Again, 13 mol of O2 gives 10 mol of H2O 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 31.25 mol will give{tex}\frac{ 10 \times 31.25 }{13} {/tex} mol of H2 O
=   24.038 mol of water
= (24.038 {tex}\times{/tex} 18)g of water
{tex}\approx{/tex} 432 g of water.

questions.question: For the reaction Fe2O3 + 3CO $\longrightarrow$ 2 Fe + 3 CO2 the volume of carbon monoxide required to reduce one mole of ferric oxide is:
questions.question_raw: For the reaction Fe2O3  + 3CO {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Fe + 3 CO2 the volume of carbon monoxide required to reduce one mole of ferric oxide is:
questions.explanation:
Based on the given equation it is clear that 1 mole of Fe2O3​ i.e ferric oxide is reduced by  3 carbon monoxide.
1 mole of any compound occupies 22.4l of volume. 
So, the volume of carbon monoxide required to reduce one mole of ferric oxide is  = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 224.4dm3 = 67.2dm3.

questions.question: For the reaction, 2 N2H4 (l) + N2O4 (l) $\to $ 3N2(g) + 4H2O(l), if 160 g of N2H4 is mixed with 160 g N2O4, the yield of H2O is:
questions.question_raw: For the reaction, 2 N2H4 (l) + N2O4 (l) {tex}\to {/tex} 3N2(g) + 4H2O(l), if 160 g of N2H4 is mixed with 160 g N2O4, the yield of H2O is:
questions.explanation:
Calculations:
Moles of N2H4
={tex}\frac{160}{32}{/tex} 
= 5 moles  
Moles of N2O4
= {tex}\frac{160}{92}{/tex}
=  1.739 moles
As per stoichiometry  of  the given reaction,
2 moles of N2H4 require 1 mole of N2O4,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 5 mole of N2H4 will need 2.5 moles of N2O4
. But we have only 1.73 mole of N2O4 so it would be consumed first.
Thus  N2O4 is the limiting reagent for this reaction.
Again, since 1 mole of N2O4 yields water
= 4 mole of water
{tex} \therefore{/tex} 1.73 mole of N2O4 will yield water
= 6.92 moles
{tex}\equiv {/tex} (6.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 18) g
=  124.56g of water

questions.question: Which of the following statements indicates that law of multiple proportion is being followed.
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements indicates that law of multiple proportion is being followed.

options.text: At constant temperature and pressure 200 mL of hydrogen will combine with 100 mL oxygen to produce 200 mL of water vapour.
When magnesium burns in oxygen, the amount of magnesium taken for the reaction is equal to the amount of magnesium in magnesium oxide formed.
Carbon forms two oxides namely CO2 and CO, where masses of oxygen which combine with fixed mass of carbon are in the simple ratio 2:1.
Sample of carbon dioxide taken from any source will always have carbon and oxygen in the simple ratio 1:2

questions.explanation:
According to law of multiple proportion, if two elements can combine to form more than one compound, the masses of one element that combine with a fixed mass of the other element, are in the ratio of small whole numbers.
e.g during the formation of CO2 and CO, if fix the mass of O then
in case of CO2  = 32 g of oxygen combine with = 12 g C
1 g of oxygen combine with = 0.375 g of C
and in case of CO = 16 g oxygen combine = 12 g of C
1 g oxygen combine = 0.75 g of C
and simplest ratio is: 0.375 : 0.75 = 1 : 2
which is according to law of multiple proportions.

questions.question: The empirical formula and molecular mass of a compound are CH2O and 180 g respectively. What will be the molecular formula of the compound?

Molecular formula = n {tex}\times{/tex} empirical formula
Empirical formula = CH2O
Empirical formula mass = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 30 g
Molecular mass = 180 g
n = {tex}\frac { \text {Molar mass} } { \text { Empirical formula mass } } = \frac { 180 } { 30 } = 6{/tex}
Therefore, n = 6
So, molecular formula = {tex}n \times C H _ { 2 } O = 6 \times C H _ { 2 } O = C _ { 6 } H _ { 12 } O _ { 6 } {/tex}

Compound Mass of A (g) Mass of B (g)
AB2 MA 2MB
A2B2 2MA 2MB

We know that,
Number of moles {tex}(n)=\frac{\text { given mass }(w)}{\text { molecular mass }(M)}{/tex}
{tex}n \times M=w{/tex}
using equation (A), it can be concluded that
{tex}5\left(M_{A}+2 M_{B}\right)=125 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{kg}{/tex} ... (i)
{tex}10\left(2 M_{A}+2 M_{B}\right)=300 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{kg}{/tex} .... (ii)
From equation (i) and (ii)
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left(M_{A}+2 M_{B}\right)}{\left(2 M_{A}+2 M_{B}\right)}=\left(\frac{125}{300}\right){/tex}
On solving the equation, we obtain
{tex}M_{A}=5 \times 10^{-3}{/tex}
and {tex}M_{B}=10 \times 10^{-3}{/tex}
So, the molar mass of A(MA) is
{tex}5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} and B(MB) is {tex}10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}

In eudiometry,
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{x} \mathrm{H}_{y}+\left(x+\frac{y}{4}\right) \mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow[{1\;atm}]{{300K}}x\;C{O_2} + \frac{y}{2}{H_2}O{/tex}
{tex}\text { 1 mol } \quad\left(x+\frac{y}{4}\right) \mathrm{mol}{/tex} x mol
{tex}\text { 1 mL } \quad\left(x+\frac{y}{4}\right) \mathrm{mL}{/tex} x mL
{tex}10 \mathrm{mL} \quad\left(x+\frac{y}{4}\right) \times 10 \mathrm{mL}{/tex} 10x mL
Given,

  1. {tex}V_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=10 x=40 \mathrm{mL} \Rightarrow x=4{/tex}
  2. {tex}V_{O_{2}}=10\left(x+\frac{y}{4}\right) m L=55 \mathrm{mL}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 10\left(4+\frac{y}{4}\right)=55{/tex} {tex}[\because x=4]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 40+\frac{y \times 10}{4}=55{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \times \frac{10}{4}=15 \Rightarrow y=15 \times \frac{4}{10}=6{/tex}
    So, the hydrocarbon (CxHy) is C4H6

questions.question: 10 mL of 1 mM surfactant solution forms a monolayer covering 0.24 cm2 on a polar substrate. If the polar head is approximated as a cube, what is its edge length?

Given, volume = 10mL
Molarity = 1 mM = 10-3M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of millimoles = 10mL {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3M = 10-2
Number of moles = 10-5
Now, number of molecules
= Number of moles {tex}\times{/tex} Avogadro's number
{tex}=10^{-5} \times 6 \times 10^{23}=6 \times 10^{18}{/tex}
Surface area occupied by {tex}6 \times 10^{18}{/tex} molecules = 0.24 cm2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Surface area occupied by 1  molecule
{tex}=\frac{0.24}{6 \times 10^{18}}=0.04 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
As it is given that polar head is approximated as cube. Thus, surface area of cube = a2, where
a = edge length
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a2 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 cm2
a = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 cm = 2pm

questions.question: 8g of NaOH is dissolved in 18 g of H2O. Mole fraction of NaOH in solution and molality (in mol kg-1) of the solution respectively are
questions.question_raw: 8g of NaOH is dissolved in 18 g of H2O. Mole fraction of NaOH in solution and molality (in mol kg-1) of the solution respectively are
questions.explanation:
Mole fraction of solute
{tex}=\frac{\text { number of moles of solute }+\text { number of moles solvent }}{\text { number of moles of solute }}{/tex}
{tex}\chi_{\text {Solute }}=\frac{n_{\text {Solute }}}{n_{\text {Solute }}+n_{\text {Solvent }}}=\frac{\frac{W_{\text {Solute }}}{M w_{\text {Solute }}}}{\frac{W_{\text {Solute }}}{M w_{\text {Solute }}}+\frac{\text { WSolvent }}{M \mu_{\text {Solvent }}}}{/tex}
Given, {tex}w_{\text {Solute }}=w_{\text {NaOH }}=8 \mathrm{g}{/tex}
{tex}M w_{\text {Solute }}=M w_{\mathrm{NaOH}}=40 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}w_{\text {Solvent }}=w_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}=18 \mathrm{g}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \chi_{\text {Solute }}=\chi_{\mathrm{NaOH}}=\frac{8 / 40}{\frac{8}{40}+\frac{18}{18}}=\frac{0.2}{0.2+1}=\frac{0.2}{1.2}=0.167{/tex}
Now, molality (m) {tex}=\frac{\text { Moles of solute }}{ \text { Mass of solvent (in }\mathrm{kg})}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{w_{\text {Solute }}}{M w_{\text {Solute }}}}{w_{\text {Solvent }}(\text { in } g)} \times 1000=\frac{\frac{8}{40}}{18} \times 1000{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{0.2}{18} \times 1000=11.11 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}{/tex}
Thus, mole fraction of NaOH in solution and molality of the solution respectively are 0.167 and 11.11 mol kg-1.

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \text { Molarity }=\frac{2.000}{0.973}=2.05 \mathrm{M}{/tex}

questions.question_raw: If 0.50 mole of BaCl2 is mixed with 0.20 mole of Na3PO4, the maximum number of moles of Ba3(PO4)2 that can be formed is
questions.explanation:
The balanced chemical reaction is
3BaCl2 + 2Na3PO4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ba3(PO4)2 + 6NaCl
In this reaction, 3 moles of BaCl2 combines with 2 moles of Na3PO4. Hence, 0.5 mole of of BaCl2 require
{tex}\frac{2}{3} \times {/tex} 0.5 = 0.33 mole of Na3PO4
Since, available Na3PO4 (0.2 mole) is less than required mole (0.33), it is the limiting reactant and would determine the amount of product Ba3(PO4)2.
{tex}\because{/tex} 2 moles of Na3PO4 gives 1 mole Ba3(PO4)2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.2 mole of Na3PO4 would give {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} 0.2 = 0.1 mole Ba3(PO4)2

100 mL of N/10 H2SO4 = 0.100 L x 0.1 N = 0.01 equivalent moles
0.02 equivalent moles of HCl will neutralise 0.02 equivalent moles of NaOH
And 0.01 equivalent moles of H2SO4 will neutralise 0.01 equivalent moles of NaOH
So unreacted equivalent moles of NaOH = 0.05 - (0.02 + 0.01) = 0.02

We have to calculate the number of molecules of water in 1.8 ml of water. As the density of water is 1 g/ml,
Therefore the mass of water in 1.8 ml of water is = Density of water X Volume of water (As Density = Mass / Volume)
= 1 X 1.8
= 1.8 g
Now the molar mass of water is 18 g. Therefore there are 6.022 X 1023 molecules of water present in 18 grams of water.
This means18 g of water = 6.022 X 1023 molecules of water
or 1 g of water = (6.022 X 1023 /  18) molecules of water
Thus 1.8 g of water = (6.022 X 1023 X 1.8) / 18 molecules of water = 0.6022 X 1023 molecules of water

questions.question: 1 mole of a mixture containing CO and CO2 requires exactly 20 g of NaOH in aqueous solution for complete neutralization. How much more of NaOH will be needed to neutralise gaseous mixture if the mixture is completely oxidised to CO2:
questions.question_raw: 1 mole of a mixture containing CO and CO2 requires exactly 20 g of NaOH in aqueous solution for complete neutralization. How much more of NaOH will be needed to neutralise gaseous mixture if the mixture is completely oxidised to CO2:
questions.explanation:
2 NAOH + CO₂ —> Na₂CO₃ + H₂O

Mole ratio: 1 : 2

We need 2 moles of NaOH to convert one mole of CO₂ to Na₂CO₃.

Moles of NaOH : 20 / 40 = 0.5 moles

Moles of CO₂ : 0.5/ 2 = 0.25

Moles of CO are: 1 - 0.25 = 0.75 moles.

Oxidation of CO :

CO + 1/2 O₂ —> CO₂

Mole ratio is 1 : 1

Moles of CO₂ formed are 0.75 moles.

Thus is half the moles of NaOH required.

NaOH required is thus :

2 × 0.75 =1.5 moles

1 mole —> 40 g

1.5 moles —>?

1.5 × 40 = 60g

60 g is required.

{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{a}{b / 2}=\frac{0.07}{0.04}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{a}{b}=\frac{0.07}{0.08}=\frac{7}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} mole ratio of C and H : 7 : 8

questions.question: The total ionic strength containing 0.1 M of CuSO4 and 0.1 of Al2(SO4)3 is:
questions.question_raw: The total ionic strength containing 0.1 M of CuSO4 and 0.1 of Al2(SO4)3 is:
questions.explanation:
One mole ofCuSO4 gives 1 mole of Cu2+ and 1 mole of {tex}\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}. Also 1 mole of Al2(SO4)3 gives 2 mole of Al3+ and 3 mole of {tex}\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}.
Ionic strength {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \Sigma c z^{2}{/tex} (where c is cone, and z is valence)
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[c z^{2}\left(\text { for } \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right)+c z^{2}\left(\text { for } \mathrm{SO}_{4 .}^{2-}\right)+c z^{2}\left(\text { for } \mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right)+c z^{2}\left(\text { for } \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[0.1 \times 2^{2}+0.1 \times 2^{2}+0.1 \times 2 \times 3^{2}+0.1 \times 3 \times 2^{2}\right]{/tex}

 Total mass of iron and oxygen in reactants = total mass of iron and oxygen in product; therefore, it follows law of conservation of mass.
 Total mass of reactants = total mass of product; therefore, law of multiple proportions is followed.
 Amount of Fe2O3 can be increased by taking any one of the reactants (iron or oxygen) in excess.
 Amount of Fe2O3 produced will decrease if the amount of any one of the reactants (iron or oxygen) is taken in excess.
questions.question_raw:

  1. Total mass of iron and oxygen in reactants = total mass of iron and oxygen in product; therefore, it follows law of conservation of mass.
  2. Total mass of reactants = total mass of product; therefore, law of multiple proportions is followed.

  4. Amount of Fe2O3 produced will decrease if the amount of any one of the reactants (iron or oxygen) is taken in excess.

Total mass of iron and oxygen in reactants = 4(55.85) + 3(32) = 319.4 g
Total mass of iron and oxygen in product = 2(2 {tex}\times{/tex} 55.85 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 8) = 319.4 g
The total mass of iron and oxygen in reactants = total mass of iron and oxygen in the product, therefore, it follows the law of conservation of mass.

A 10 mg effervescent tablet containing sodium bicarbonate and oxalic acid releases 0.25 mL of CO2 at T = 298.15K and P = 1 bar. If molar volume of CO2 is 25.0 L under such condition, what is the percentage of sodium bicarbonate in each tablet?

questions.question: A sample of NaClO3 is converted by heat to NaCl with a loss of 0.16 g of oxygen. The residue is dissolved in water and precipitated as AgCl. The mass of AgCl (in g) obtained will be: (Given: Molar mass of AgCl = 143.5 gmol-1)
questions.question_raw: A sample of NaClO3 is converted by heat to NaCl with a loss of 0.16 g of oxygen. The residue is dissolved in water and precipitated as AgCl. The mass of AgCl (in g) obtained will be: (Given: Molar mass of AgCl = 143.5 gmol-1)
questions.explanation:
No. of moles of oxygen in 0.16 g of oxygen molecule
{tex}=\frac{0.16 \mathrm{~g}}{32 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}{/tex} = 0.005 mol
2NaClO3 {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta }{/tex} 2 NaCl + 3O2
According to the reaction,
3 moles of O2 = 2 moles of NaCl = 2 moles of AgCl
Molar mass of AgCl = 143.5 g/mol
0.005 moles of O2 will ppt. = 0.005 {tex}\times \frac{2}{3}{/tex} moles AgCl
= 0.0033 moles of AgCl
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of AgCl (in g) obtained will be
= 143.5 g/mol {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0033 moles = 048 g

questions.explanation:

Assuming BaCl2{tex}\cdot{/tex}xH2O as hydrate

Grass molecular wt. of BaCl2 = 208 g/mol

% of H2O in BaCl2{tex}\cdot{/tex}xH2O = {tex}\frac{18 x}{208+18 x} \times{/tex} 100
Thus, {tex}\frac{18 x}{208+18 x} \times{/tex} 100 = 14.75
On solving x = 2
Hence, the formula of hydrated salt is BaCl2{tex}\cdot{/tex}2H2O

questions.question: If the density of a solution is 4.125 g mL-1, the mass of 2.50 mL solution in CORRECT significant figures is:
questions.question_raw: If the density of a solution is 4.125 g mL-1, the mass of 2.50 mL solution in CORRECT significant figures is:
questions.explanation:
Density of solution = 4.125 g mL-1
Volume of solution = 2.50 mL
Density ol a solution = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of solution }}{\text { Volume of solution }}{/tex}
4.125 g ML-1 = {tex}\frac x{2.50 mL}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 4.125 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.50
x = 10.3125 g
The result of a multiplication or division should contain the same number of significant figures as present in the least precise term of the calculation.
Here, the least precise term = 2.50
Hence, the required answer should contain three significant digits.
i.e., 10.3125 should be rounded off to 10.3 g.

= {tex}\frac{4}{3} \times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (7)3 cm3
Density = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of sphere = Density {tex}\times{/tex} Volume of sphere
= 1.4 {tex}\times \frac{4}{3} \times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (7)3 g = 2010 g
Percentage of iron in the sphere = 56 W/W
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 100 g of sphere contains 56 g of iron.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2010 g contains {tex}\frac{56 \times 2010}{100} \mathrm{~g}{/tex} of iron.
Now, Atomic mass of iron = 56 g mol-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No. of moles of iron = {tex}\frac{56 \times 2010}{56 \times 100}{/tex} = 20.1 mol

questions.question: If 1021 molecules are removed from 400 mg of CO2, then the number of moles of CO2 left are ________.
questions.question_raw: If 1021 molecules are removed from 400 mg of CO2, then the number of moles of CO2 left are ________.
questions.explanation:
400 mg of CO2 = 0.4 g of CO2
{tex}\because{/tex} 44 g of CO2 = 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.4 g of CO2 = {tex}\frac{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{44} \times{/tex} 0.4
= 5.47 {tex}\times{/tex} 1021 molecules
Now 1021 molecules are removed,
So, remaining molecules
= 5.47 {tex}\times{/tex} 1021 - 1021
= 1021 (5.47 - 1)
= 4.47 {tex}\times{/tex} 1021 molecules
Now, 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules = 1 mole
4.47 {tex}\times{/tex} 1021 molecules = {tex}\frac{1 \times 4.47 \times 10^{21}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{/tex}
= 74.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 moles

questions.question: Elements M and N form two different compounds. In the first compound, 0.324 g of M is combined with 0.471 g of N. In the second compound, 0.117 g of M is combined with 0.509 g of N. The weights of N that combine with the same weight of M are in the ratio of ________.
questions.question_raw: Elements M and N form two different compounds. In the first compound, 0.324 g of M is combined with 0.471 g of N. In the second compound, 0.117 g of M is combined with 0.509 g of N. The weights of N that combine with the same weight of M are in the ratio of ________.
questions.explanation:
This is based on law of multiple proportions.
1st compound:
0.324 g M combines with 0.471 g N.
2nd compound:
0.117 g M combines with 0.509 g N
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.324 g M combines with {tex}\frac{0.509 \times 0.324}{0.117}{/tex} = 1.4 g N
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ratio of weights of N that combine with equal weight of M = 0.471 : 1.4 = 1 : 3

questions.question: Caffeine (chemical structure given below) is a stimulant that binds to our brain’s adenosine receptors which temporarily makes us feel awake and energetic. Caffeine is found in tea, coffee, cocoa, energy drinks and other soft drinks. 100 g of coffee contains 40 mg of caffeine, while 100 g of cola contains 8 mg. If person A chooses to drink 100 g coffee and person B drinks an equivalent amount of cola, how much excess of caffeine molecules did A consume?
questions.question_raw: Caffeine (chemical structure given below) is a stimulant that binds to our brain’s adenosine receptors which temporarily makes us feel awake and energetic. Caffeine is found in tea, coffee, cocoa, energy drinks and other soft drinks. 100 g of coffee contains 40 mg of caffeine, while 100 g of cola contains 8 mg. If person A chooses to drink 100 g coffee and person B drinks an equivalent amount of cola, how much excess of caffeine molecules did A consume?
questions.explanation:
Molecular formula of caffeine = C8H10N4O2
Molar mass of caffeine = 194.12 g
100 g coffee = 40 mg caffeine = 0.04 g caffeine
Number of molecules of caffeine in 100 g coffee
= 0.04 g caffeine {tex}\times \frac{1 \text { mol caffeine }}{194.12 \text { g caffeine }} \times \frac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text { mol ecules }}{1 \text { mol caffeine }}{/tex}
= 1.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 molecules of caffeine
Number of molecules of caffeine in 100 g cola
= 0.008 g caffeine {tex}\times \frac{1 \text { mol caffeine }}{194.12 \text { g caffeine }} \times \frac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text { mol ecules }}{1 \text { mol caffeine }}{/tex}
= 2.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 1019 molecules of caffeine
Excess caffeine molecules consumed by "A"
= (1.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 - 2.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 1019) molecules
= 0.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 caffeine molecules

 density of water to be 1 g/cm3 and
 water molecule to be spherical.

What is the diameter of a water molecule?
Assume:

  1. density of water to be 1 g/cm3 and
  2. water molecule to be spherical.
questions.explanation:
1 mole of water = 18 g = 18 cm3
= 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of H2O ({tex}\because{/tex} density of H2O = 1 g/cm3)
Thus, 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules have volume = 18 cm3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 molecule of H2O have volume = {tex}\frac {18}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{/tex}
= 2.989 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-23 cm3
As water molecule is assumed to be spherical if R is its radius, then its volume will be:
{tex}\frac 43 \pi{/tex}R3 = 2.989 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-23 cm3
R3 = 7.133 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24
R = 1.925 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 cm
Diameter of water molecule
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.925 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 cm
= 3.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 cm

questions.question: Suppose the elements X and Y combine to form two compounds XY2 and X3Y2. When 0.1 mole of XY2 weighs 10 g and 0.05 mole of X3Y2 weighs 9 g, the atomic weights of X and Y are ________.

questions.explanation:
0.1 mol of XY2 = 10 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 mol of XY2 = 100 g
i.e, Molecular weight of XY2 = 100
0.05 mol of X3Y2 = 9 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 mol of X3Y2 = 180 g
i.e., Molecular weight of X3Y2 = 180
Let atomic weights of X and Y be x and y respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + 2y = 100 ...(i)
3x + 2y = 180 ...(ii)
Substrating (i) from (ii),
2x = 180 - 100
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 40
Substituting x = 40 in (i),
40 + 2y = 100
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = 30

{tex}\therefore{/tex} xsolute = {tex}\frac {18}{118}{/tex} = 0.15

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total number of molecules present in the container is

= 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022

questions.question: A beaker contains 100 mL of 2.0 M aqueous solution of NaCl. The mass of AgCl precipitated when this NaCl solution is added to a solution containing 3.4 g of AgNO3 is _______ . [Atomic mass: Ag = 108, Cl = 35.5, Na = 23, N = 14, O = 16]
questions.question_raw: A beaker contains 100 mL of 2.0 M aqueous solution of NaCl. The mass of AgCl precipitated when this NaCl solution is added to a solution containing 3.4 g of AgNO3 is _______ . [Atomic mass: Ag = 108, Cl = 35.5, Na = 23, N = 14, O = 16]
questions.explanation:
Since the quantities of two different reactants are given, first find the limiting reagent. For this, convert the given concentration of NaCl solution and the mass of AgNO3 to moles and then use reaction stoichiometry.
The molarity of NaCl solution is 2.0 M. i.e., 1000 ml. contains 2 mol NaCl.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 100 mL solution contains 0.2 mol NaCl.
Now, 3.4 g AgNO3 = {tex}\frac {3.4}{170}{/tex} = 0.02 mol
Reaction involved:
NaCl + AgNO3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} AgCl + NaNO3
According to reaction stoichiometry,
1 mol of NaCl = 1 mol of AgNO3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.02 mol NaCl will react with 0.02 mol of AgNO3
Hence, AgNO3 is the limiting reagent.
Now, calculate the amount of product formed from the moles of the limiting reagent.
According to reaction stoichiometry,
1 mol of AgNO3 = 1 mol of AgCl
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.02 mol of AgNO3 = 0.02 mol of AgCl
= 0.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 143.5
= 2.87 g AgCl
